summary:
Ka-Ne are the anti-vital-double forms of Unlife: native negative-vital beings organized around the inversion, hollowing, theft, or contradiction of a creature’s sustaining self-image.

A Ka-Ne is not merely a shadow, ghost, reflection, duplicate, fetch, or undead remnant. It is the unliving pressure left where a being’s inward continuity has been denied. It forms around the absence of the sustaining “I”: the name that no longer fits, the reflection that no longer agrees, the honor that no longer feeds the soul, the remembered face that has become hostile, the double that proves the original is incomplete.

Where many living creatures are sustained by breath, food, memory, social recognition, and inner coherence, Ka-Ne persist by negating those supports. They do not simply kill identity. They make identity fail to nourish its bearer. A victim may still have a name, face, history, body, and reputation, but none of these restore wholeness. The Ka-Ne grows in the gap.

The phylum is therefore best understood as Unlife of the inverted vital double: beings that feed on anti-personhood, broken self-continuity, false reflection, denied names, inverted honor, and the collapse between a creature and the image that should sustain it.
tax:
Ka-Ne belongs beneath Unlife because its members are native inverse-vital beings rather than transformed living creatures. They are not the dead, not ghosts, not possessed reflections, and not ordinary shadows cast by a body. They arise when the sustaining pattern of personhood is contradicted strongly enough to become an unliving counter-pattern.

This taxon should be used when the primary mechanism is the negation of a vital double, selfhood, name, honor, reflection, or inward continuity. A creature whose body dies and leaves behind a soul-remnant belongs elsewhere. A creature whose corpse rises belongs elsewhere. A creature whose hunger is empowered by lack belongs elsewhere. A Ka-Ne is defined by the failure of the self to sustain itself.

Common origins include stolen names, broken oaths that sever self-recognition, mirrors used to imprison identity, masks worn until the wearer is hollow, ceremonial shaming that strips a person of inward status, false doubles accepted as true, reflections fed instead of bodies, erased lineages, contradictory memories, and acts that make a creature unable to recognize its own life as its own.

The outward shape is not taxonomically decisive. A Ka-Ne may look like a reflection, shadow, twin, mask, portrait, echo, silhouette, name-mark, empty robe, or walking negative of its subject. The placement test is whether the entity grows by making identity, recognition, and self-continuity fail.
ap:
Ka-Ne often appear as bodies that are almost correct. Their wrongness is usually precise rather than monstrous. A face may be familiar but emotionally reversed. A reflection may blink late. A shadow may move with perfect etiquette while the body trembles. A portrait may show the sitter as they would have been if they had betrayed themselves sooner. A name may appear written backward on the tongue, palm, mirror, or threshold.

Some Ka-Ne have no stable body until witnessed. They may first appear as a second shadow in candlelight, a figure behind the viewer in polished metal, a voice speaking from the wrong side of the skull, a mask that fits too well, or a formal title spoken by someone who should not know it. Others manifest as pale doubles, blackened twins, skinless silhouettes, faceless courtiers, inverted heralds, or child-sized copies that carry an adult’s missing certainty.

Their bodies often display signs of anti-personhood: blank eyes with the victim’s memories moving behind them, lips that speak only names, hands stained with ink but unable to write truth, reversed scars, hollow crowns, broken seals, missing reflections, or clothing appropriate to a role the victim failed or refused. A Ka-Ne tied to shame may look over-dressed and faceless. One tied to stolen identity may look healthier than its original. One tied to self-erasure may be almost invisible except for its outline.

Field signs include mirrors showing a room with one extra person, signatures appearing in unfamiliar hands, masks becoming warm when unworn, titles sounding accusatory, old names causing nausea, shadows falling toward absent light, portraits aging at the wrong pace, and witnesses forgetting which version of a person entered first.
eco:
Ka-Ne persist where identity is made unstable, split, denied, counterfeited, or ritually inverted. Suitable habitats include mirror halls, oath chambers, masked courts, execution rooms, abandoned theatres, sealed archives, naming houses, dishonored shrines, prison cells where names were replaced by numbers, dueling grounds, portrait galleries, inheritance halls, confession rooms, and places where a person was publicly unmade.

They do not reproduce biologically. A Ka-Ne forms when a self-sustaining identity is damaged in a way that continues to produce negative vitality. This may happen through repeated denial, magical duplication, oath-breaking, ritual disgrace, name-theft, false inheritance, mirror-binding, possession of a role without the self to bear it, or the acceptance of a counterfeit as the true person.

Lesser Ka-Ne may begin as signs rather than creatures: a reflection that disobeys, a name that will not stay written, a shadow that bows to the wrong master, a mask that changes expression, or a title that follows a victim from mouth to mouth. Stronger specimens can become independent hunters, false companions, household infiltrators, courtly parasites, or battlefield doubles that make allies hesitate.

Their ecology is fed by contradiction. The more a community insists that the false self is true, the stronger a Ka-Ne may become. The more a victim denies a true name, hides a guilt, abandons an oath, or performs a role without inward continuity, the more stable the entity becomes. Conversely, acts of truthful recognition, restored naming, accepted guilt, repaired oaths, broken false masks, and reconciliation between body, name, and deed can diminish them.

Ka-Ne are dangerous because they often thrive in civilized order. A famine, tomb, or battlefield is not required. A court, guild, monastery, academy, theatre, household, military command, or noble line can produce them if identity is treated as costume, leverage, punishment, or property long enough.
beh:
Ka-Ne behavior is corrective in a hostile, inverted sense. They seek to prove that the victim is not the victim, that the name belongs elsewhere, that the reflection is more true than the body, that the role has outlived the person, or that the mask deserves the face beneath it.

Some stalk a single subject. They imitate posture, handwriting, voice, manners, or memories, not always perfectly, but convincingly enough to disturb witnesses. Others do not replace a person directly; instead, they erode the person’s ability to be believed. A Ka-Ne may arrive one step ahead, sign documents first, receive greetings first, cast the expected shadow, or answer to the name before the victim can speak.

Many are patient. They prefer doubt, shame, and contradiction over immediate violence. A Ka-Ne may cause companions to misremember conversations, make mirrors unreliable, force a victim to hear their own voice denying them, or appear only when the victim acts against their claimed principles. The goal is not always death. Often it is vacancy: a living body that can no longer be nourished by its own identity.

Toward groups, Ka-Ne exploit hierarchy and recognition. They appear where titles matter, where masks are worn, where oaths bind, where bloodlines rule, where disgrace exiles, or where records define personhood. They can be especially dangerous in courts, armies, temples, guilds, and noble houses because these places can mistake formal recognition for truth.

A Ka-Ne may be bargained with, but such bargains are perilous. It may offer to carry guilt, preserve a public face, take punishment, impersonate courage, remember what the victim cannot bear, or erase an unwanted name. The price is usually deeper division between the creature and its sustaining self.
traitsText:
**Type Guidance**. Use Unlife as the preferred type. If a ruleset lacks Unlife, use Undead with a native-unlife, negative-vital, reflection, shadow, or identity-bound subtype. Ka-Ne are mechanically adjacent to shadows, mirrors, fetches, haunts, and doubles, but they are not automatically ghosts, undead remnants, or summoned duplicates.

**Native Counter-Self**. A Ka-Ne is not the victim’s soul, corpse, or ordinary reflection. It is a native inverse-vital counter-pattern formed around self-negation. Even when bound to a subject, it should remain taxonomically distinct from that subject.

**Identity Anchor**. Every Ka-Ne should have a defined anchor: name, reflection, mask, portrait, title, oath, signature, lineage, shadow, role, reputation, memory, or public recognition. Its abilities and weaknesses should flow from that anchor.

**Recognition Pressure**. Ka-Ne often become stronger when others accept the false self, repeat the wrong name, honor the wrong title, obey the mask, or deny the original. They become weaker when truth is spoken and socially or ritually confirmed.

**Senses**. Darkvision is common. Many Ka-Ne can detect named creatures, spoken titles, reflective surfaces, masks, oathbreakers, disguised creatures, creatures suffering shame or identity loss, and written names connected to their anchor.

**Defenses**. Incorporeality, concealment in dim light, resistance to cold or negative energy, channel resistance, immunity to fatigue, disease, poison, sleep, mind-affecting effects, or nonlethal damage may fit many descendants. These defenses should be suppressible by correct naming, broken mirrors, removed masks, fulfilled oaths, public recognition, or reconciliation with the denied self.

**Weakness Pattern**. A Ka-Ne should have a truth-based or recognition-based vulnerability. Examples include hearing its subject’s true name spoken by a rightful witness, seeing the original accept a denied deed, having its mask removed, being reflected in a flawless surface, losing a stolen title, being confronted with an unaltered record, or standing before someone who knows both the false and true self.

**Skills**. Bluff, Disguise, Stealth, Sense Motive, Perception, Intimidate, Perform, Knowledge nobility, Knowledge religion, Knowledge local, Linguistics, and Use Magic Device are common directions. Bonuses should follow the anchor: mirror-forms excel at ambush and misdirection, name-forms at Linguistics, mask-forms at Disguise, title-forms at social manipulation.

**Environment**. Mirror halls, courts, archives, theatres, oath chambers, prisons, military halls, portrait galleries, noble houses, masked festivals, confession rooms, inheritance chambers, dueling grounds, and any site of public disgrace or identity transfer.

**Organization**. Solitary, pair, reflection, masked set, false household, name-knot, shadow court, oath-chain, portrait line, duplicate retinue, or counter-lineage.
abilities:
**Counter-Self Manifestation** (Su). A Ka-Ne can manifest as a hostile counter-pattern of a creature’s identity. This manifestation may resemble a shadow, reflection, double, mask, title-bearing figure, or named echo. The manifestation is strongest while the relevant identity contradiction remains unresolved.

**Name-Leech** (Su). The Ka-Ne weakens a creature by drawing force from its name or title. The victim may take penalties to Charisma-based checks, saving throws against fear or compulsion, caster level checks tied to named authority, or attempts to convince others of their identity. Speaking the correct name with rightful recognition may suppress the effect.

**False Reflection** (Su). The Ka-Ne can act through mirrors, polished metal, still water, glass, portraits, or other reflective symbols. It may observe, whisper, manifest partially, distort appearances, or cause a reflected image to behave independently. Breaking the surface may disrupt the effect but should not destroy the Ka-Ne unless the surface is its defined anchor.

**Mask Claim** (Su). A Ka-Ne tied to a mask, role, or public face can gain power when a creature performs falsely. While the victim knowingly maintains a false identity, abandoned oath, stolen title, or concealed disgrace, the Ka-Ne may gain bonuses against that victim or become harder to perceive as hostile.

**Doubt Aura** (Su). Living creatures near a strong Ka-Ne may become uncertain of names, loyalties, memories, titles, or the order of events. This may impose penalties on Sense Motive, Will saves, identification checks, social checks, or teamwork. Severe versions may cause confusion, shaken, fascinated, or compelled silence, but the effect should remain tied to identity instability rather than generic madness.

**Stolen Mien** (Sp or Su). The Ka-Ne may imitate a subject’s voice, face, handwriting, scent, shadow, or social presence. The imitation need not be physically perfect; it attacks recognition. Creatures who rely on formal signs, titles, badges, or reputation may be more vulnerable than those with intimate knowledge.

**Self-Denial Wound** (Su). When a victim acts against a sworn identity, denies a true name, betrays a defining oath, or accepts a false self as necessary, the Ka-Ne may deal negative energy damage, Charisma damage, Wisdom damage, nonlethal damage, or impose fatigue, shaken, or sickened conditions. The trigger must be meaningful and should be defined clearly in the lower entry.

**True Recognition Diminishment** (Su). When the Ka-Ne is confronted by correct recognition, it loses coherence. Effects may include forced visibility, loss of incorporeality, reduced defenses, inability to use disguise or reflection abilities, damage, staggered condition, or temporary banishment. The recognition must be more than a password; it should involve truthful naming, restored role, accepted guilt, fulfilled oath, or witness-backed identity.

**Hollow Double** (Su). Strong Ka-Ne can produce or inhabit a duplicate-like body. This hollow double may infiltrate a household, court, military unit, or adventuring company. It may not need to defeat the original directly; it only needs others to treat the double as more valid. Destroying the double may not end the Ka-Ne unless the underlying identity contradiction is repaired.
bg:
Ka-Ne give Unlife a place for monsters of anti-personhood rather than hunger, death, or simple shadow. They are useful when the horror is not “the dead return” but “the self no longer sustains the living.”

They fit stories about stolen names, false heirs, masks that become truer than faces, oathbreakers pursued by their abandoned selves, reflections that accuse, portraits that replace, doubles that inherit, and titles that devour their bearers. They are especially effective in campaigns where social identity has mechanical and narrative weight: noble rank, divine office, guild standing, military command, bloodline, pact, vow, true name, or heroic reputation.

The practical field lesson is that violence alone may not solve a Ka-Ne infestation. A sword can cut a shadow-form. A spell can shatter a mirror. A banishment can clear a room. But if the false name remains honored, the stolen title remains legal, the mask remains enthroned, or the original continues denying the truth that split them, the Ka-Ne can return.

Correct response depends on the anchor. Restore the name. Break the false reflection. Remove the mask. Fulfill or renounce the oath honestly. Correct the record. Publicly recognize the true bearer. Let the victim confess what the Ka-Ne has been using as a wound. In some cases, the monster is defeated not by proving that the double is false, but by making the original whole enough that the double has no lack to inhabit.
note:
Theme: inverted vital double.
Name root: Egyptian ka, the vital force or life-power of the person, which could receive offerings through mortuary practice. Egyptian sources describe the ka accessing offerings through the tomb’s false door and receiving food, drink, and essentials.

Description: Ka-Negatives are unliving anti-doubles: not ghosts of a person, but negative-vital counterparts to the concept of sustaining life-force. They seek identity, memory, honor, and nourishment, but these things reduce them; anonymity, dishonor, forgetfulness, and deprivation make them stronger.

Supportive argument: This branch gives Unlife a mythic “anti-soul” or “anti-vital double” without making it undead. It is especially useful for entities mistaken for ghosts, ancestral spirits, or hungry dead.
